chunk_summary: This section discusses low-resource diagnostic approaches to anemia and micronutrient deficiencies in vegetarian-fed children, emphasizing cost-effective testing, therapeutic trials, and clinical guidelines for iron and vitamin B12 deficiencies.
learning_objectives: Understand the low-cost diagnostic approach to anemia in vegetarian-fed children.
Identify key clinical signs and lab findings for iron and vitamin B12 deficiencies.
Apply therapeutic trials of oral iron as both a diagnostic and treatment tool.
Explain the importance of hemoglobin thresholds in protecting neurodevelopment.
key_facts: A CBC with indices is the most informative low-cost test for anemia diagnosis.
Microcytosis with high milk intake and low pulses/legumes suggests iron deficiency anemia (IDA).
A hemoglobin rise of >1 g/dL within 4 weeks of oral iron therapy supports IDA diagnosis.
Macrocytosis (MCV >110) and hypersegmented neutrophils suggest vitamin B12 deficiency.
WHO hemoglobin thresholds are used unless national guidelines specify otherwise.
reference_quote: Indian guidance supports a therapeutic trial of oral iron as both treatment and diagnostic test; diagnosis is supported when guideline-dose iron produces a hemoglobin rise of more than 1 g/dL within about 4 weeks in mild anemia, or within about 2 weeks in more severe anemia.
document_key_topics: Iron deficiency anemia in vegetarian-fed children
Vitamin B12 deficiency and clinical signs
Importance of hemoglobin thresholds in children
